title: Mishima line inlay on air-dry clay
subtitle: Incised lines filled with coloured slip for a two-tone inlaid surface
excerpt: Mishima is a surface decoration technique in which lines are incised into leather-hard clay and then filled with slip of a contrasting colour. Once dry, the excess slip is scraped back to reveal the lines flush with the surrounding surface. This version adapts the technique for air-dry clay using acrylic-tinted slip.
sourceNotes: Mishima is a Korean surface decoration technique (Joseon dynasty, 14th-16th centuries) used on celadon ceramics. The technique migrated into Japanese studio ceramics. The name references a Japanese town associated with the decorative tradition. This tutorial adapts the incise-and-fill principle for air-dry clay.

content.text: line inlay works well on air-dry clay. Cut the lines when the clay is

. Let it firm. Scrape back to reveal the lines. The name comes from a Korean tradition of white slip set into grey celadon. This tutorial uses the same cut-and-fill idea in acrylic-tinted slip on air-dry clay. Same idea, same look, with adapted materials.

Mishima works on any flat or gently curved air-dry clay surface at leather-hard stage. Ideal for tiles, slab pieces, pinch-pot exteriors, and coil-built forms that have reached the right dryness. The incised lines must be at least 1 mm wide and 1-1.5 mm deep to hold the slip cleanly.

Use a needle tool, the point of a fettling knife, or a dedicated mishima tool (a bent needle tip set in a handle). Draw the design into the leather-hard clay with firm, smooth strokes. The lines should be 1-1.5 mm wide and cut cleanly through the clay surface without tearing. Sketching the design lightly with a pencil first is useful on complex geometric patterns.

For white-on-grey inlay on grey air-dry clay: thin a small amount of white air-dry clay in water to a thick, brushable consistency. For black inlay: add a small amount of black acrylic paint to the base slip. The contrasting slip should be the consistency of single cream: thin enough to fill the lines fully, not so thin it runs over the surface.

generously over the incised area, pushing it into the lines with the brush tip. Overfill: the lines should be full and the surrounding surface lightly coated. Let this first fill dry for 10-15 minutes.
Slip shrinks as it dries. Apply a second coat over the lines once the first is surface-dry. This ensures the lines are fully filled with no sunken centres after scraping.

Wait until the filled slip has dried to a near-leather-hard state: the surface looks lighter in colour and is firm to the touch but not brittle. Scrape the excess slip off the surrounding clay surface using the flat face of a wooden rib, holding it at a shallow angle and working in long, even strokes. The lines should appear as the scraping clears the surface clay. Continue scraping until the slip in the lines is flush with the surrounding surface.
What it looks like when it is right

After scraping, the lines read as a crisp two-tone pattern flush with the clay surface. The surrounding clay is not smeared or scratched. Once the piece reaches bone-dry, the contrast between the inlaid slip and the body clay becomes more pronounced as both lighten to their dry colour.
Scraping too early. The filled slip scrapes cleanly only when it has firmed sufficiently; if the slip is still wet, it smears into the surrounding surface rather than lifting away.

Scraping too late. Slip that has dried to bone-dry resists the rib and may take the surrounding clay surface with it. The correct window is firm but not brittle, similar to the leather-hard stage of the clay body itself.

items.fix: Leave for a further 20 minutes and try again
items.cause: Slip not firm enough when scraping began

items.fix: Apply a further fill, wait for it to firm, and scrape again
items.cause: Slip shrinkage left gaps in the lines

items.fix: The correct moment is leather-hard: firm, not soft; the needle cuts a clean groove without dragging
items.cause: Lines were incised when the clay was too soft (smearing) or too dry (tearing)
